name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَقَالَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite. He said, ‘I am indeed an apostle of the Lord of all the worlds.’ (46)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
But when he brought them Our signs, they indeed laughed at them. (47)
```

```arabic
وَمَا نُرِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ إِلَّا هِىَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا ۖ وَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
We did not show them a sign but it was greater than the other, and We visited on them punishment so that they might come back. (48)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَ ٱلسَّاحِرُ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ (٤٩)
```

```english
They would say, ‘O magician! Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you [to remove this scourge]. We will indeed be guided [when it is removed].’ (49)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (٥٠)
```

```english
But when We lifted the punishment from them, behold, they would break their pledge. (50)
```

```arabic
وَنَادَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى قَوْمِهِۦ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِى مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِىٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٥١)
```

```english
And Pharaoh proclaimed to his people, saying, ‘O my people! Do not the kingdom of Egypt and these rivers that run at my feet belong to me? Do you not perceive? (51)
```

```arabic
أَمْ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ (٥٢)
```

```english
Am I not better than this wretch who cannot even speak clearly? (52)
```

```arabic
فَلَوْلَآ أُلْقِىَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِّن ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ (٥٣)
```

```english
Why no bracelets of gold have been thrown to him, nor any angels accompany him as escorts?’ (53)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُۥ فَأَطَاعُوهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٥٤)
```

```english
Thus did he mislead his people and they obeyed him. Indeed, they were a transgressing lot. (54)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ ءَاسَفُونَا ٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥٥)
```

```english
So when they roused Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. (55)
```

```arabic
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ سَلَفًا وَمَثَلًا لِّلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
Thus We made them the vanguard and an example for posterity. (56)
```

## (Commentary)

After mentioning their rebellion (الطغيان) — *their exceeding of limits in disbelief and disobedience* — following His granting them enjoyment of His blessings, and their dismissal of the truth brought by a manifest messenger as sorcery, and their saying: **"Why was this Quran not sent down to some great man from the two cities?"** (43:31), thereby preferring a man over the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) based on the abundance of his wealth, He sets forth for them a parable through the story of Moses (عليه السلام), Pharaoh, and his people. This occurred when Allah sent him to them with His dazzling signs (الآيات), yet they laughed at them and mocked them. Pharaoh, in his address to his people, argued that he was better than Moses because of his sovereignty over Egypt and the rivers flowing beneath him. Thus, he incited them to levity (استخفهم) — *he manipulated their intellects to make them view the truth as light and insignificant* — and they obeyed him. The end of their arrogance (الاستكبار) — *the haughty refusal to submit to the truth* — was that Allah took vengeance upon them and drowned them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَى بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَى فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَائِهِ فَقَالَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** — **"Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite. He said, ‘I am indeed the apostle of the Lord of all the worlds.’"** (43:46). The letter *lam* in **لَقَدْ** is for an oath (القسم), and the *ba* in His saying **بِآيَاتِنَا** denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة), and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ بِآيَاتِنَا إِذَا هُمْ مِنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ** — **"But when he brought them Our signs, behold, they laughed at them."** (43:47). What is meant by his bringing the signs is the manifestation of miracles (المعجزات) to demonstrate his messengership (الرسالة). The laughter intended here is the laughter of mockery (الاستهزاء) out of disdain for the signs.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا نُرِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ إِلَّا هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا** — **"We did not show them any sign but it was greater than its predecessor..."** (43:48). The "sister" (الأخت) means the peer or like. His saying **هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا** is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that every single one of them reached the ultimate degree in demonstrating the truthfulness of the messengership. The clause **وَمَا نُرِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ** is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the pronoun in **مِنْهَا**. The meaning is: when he brought them the miracles, they laughed at them, while the situation was that each of them was perfect and complete in its miraculous nature and indication, without any deficiency or shortcoming.

And His saying: **وَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ بِالْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** — **"...and We seized them with punishment so that they might come back."** (43:48). That is, in the hope that they would turn back from their arrogance to accepting his message. The punishment (العذاب) with which they were seized refers to the signs of the plague (الرجز) sent upon them, such as the years of famine, the shortage of fruits, the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, and the blood—distinct signs (آيات مفصلات) as mentioned in Surah al-A'raf.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا السَّاحِرُ ادْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ** — **"They said, ‘O magician! Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. We will indeed be guided.’"** (43:49). The word *ma* in **بِمَا عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ** is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning "by His covenant with you." This refers to His covenant to remove the punishment from them if they believed, as has been said, or to answer his prayer when he supplicates, as some have suggested.

Their address **يَا أَيُّهَا السَّاحِرُ** (O magician) was a speech of mockery arising from their arrogance. Similarly, they said, "Invoke your Lord," and did not say "Invoke our Lord" or "Invoke Allah" out of pride. The meaning is that they requested him to supplicate for the removal of the punishment from them and promised him that they would be guided.

It has been said that the meaning of "magician" (الساحر) in their convention was "scholar" (العالم), and that the magician was great in their eyes and they honored him, so it was not a term of dispraise. However, this is not the case; rather, they were mocking in their arrogance, as evidenced by their saying: "Invoke your Lord for us."

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ** — **"But when We removed the punishment from them, behold, they broke their promise."** (43:50). Breaking (النكث) is the violation of a covenant (العهد) and the breaking of a promise. Their promise was their saying: "We will indeed be guided."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَادَى فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ** — **"Pharaoh proclaimed among his people, saying, ‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me and these rivers that flow at my feet? Do you not perceive?"** (43:51). That is, he called out to them while he was among them. The word **قَالَ** (he said) is separated (فصل) because it stands as an answer to a question, as if it were asked: "What did he say?" and it was replied: "He said such-and-such."

His saying: **وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي** (and these rivers that flow beneath me) means beneath my palace or from my garden in which my palace stands, elevated and high in construction. The clause **وَهَذِهِ الْأَنْهَارُ** is either a circumstantial clause or is coordinated (معطوف) with **مُلْكُ مِصْرَ** (the kingdom of Egypt), and the phrase **تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي** is a circumstantial description of the rivers. The rivers refer to the branches of the Nile.

His saying: **أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ** (Do you not perceive?) carries the meaning of repeating the previous interrogative in his saying: "Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me..."

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هَذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ** — **"Am I not better than this one who is a lowly wretch and can hardly speak plainly?"** (43:52). The lowly (المهين) is the contemptible and weak, from *mahanah* (المهانة) meaning insignificance (الحقارة). He intends by "lowly" Moses (عليه السلام) due to his poverty and the raggedness of his state.

His saying: **وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ** (and can hardly speak plainly) means he does not express his intent clearly. Perhaps he described Moses (عليه السلام) this way based on how he was before his messengership, but Allah removed that from him due to His saying: **"He said, ‘You have been granted your request, O Moses!’"** (20:36) after his (عليه السلام) saying: **"And untie the knot from my tongue, so that they might understand my speech."** (20:27-28).

Regarding the word **أَمْ** (or/rather) at the beginning of the verse **أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ**, it is either disjunctive (منقطعة) to affirm his previous speech, meaning: "Rather, I am better than Moses because he is such-and-such," or it is conjunctive (متصلة), where one side of the hesitation is omitted along with the interrogative *hamzah*, the implied meaning being: "Is this one better or am I better...?" In *al-Majma'*, it is said: "Sibawayh and al-Khalil said: 'I' (أنا) is coordinated by *am* to 'Do you not perceive?' because the meaning of 'I am better' is the meaning of 'or do you perceive?' It is as if he said: 'Do you not perceive or do you perceive?' because if they say to him: 'You are better than him,' they have become 'perceiving' in his view." End quote. That is, placing **أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ** in the position of "or do you perceive" is the placement of the effect (المسبب) in the position of the cause (السبب) or vice versa.

In any case, the reference to Moses with "this" (هذا) without mentioning him by name is for belittlement (التحقير), and describing him as **الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ** is for belittlement and to indicate his lack of superiority.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَوْ لَا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ** — **"Why have not gold bracelets been cast upon him, or why have not the angels accompanied him in a procession?"** (43:53). Bracelets (الأسورة) is the plural of *siwar* (سوار). Al-Raghib said: "It is the Arabized form of *dastwarah*." They said: "It was their custom that when they made a man a leader, they would deck him with a gold bracelet and place a gold collar around his neck." So the meaning is: "If he were a messenger and led the people by that, gold bracelets would have been cast upon him."

His saying: **أَوْ جَاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ** (or why have not the angels accompanied him in a procession). It appears that accompaniment (الاقتران) means coming together (التقارن), like *istibaq* and *istiwa'* mean competing and being equal. The meaning is the angels coming with him in close proximity to testify to his messengership. This statement is among those repeated by the tongues of those who denied the messengers, such as their saying: **"Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner with him?"** (25:7).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ فَأَطاعُوهُ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ** — **"So he incited his people to levity and they obeyed him. Indeed they were a transgressing lot."** (43:54). That is, he made light of the intellects and minds of his people. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — **"So when they provoked Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all."** (43:55). Provoking wrath (الإيساف) means causing anger (الإغضاب). That is, when they angered Us by their transgression, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. Anger (الغضب) from Allah is the will to punish (إرادة العقوبة).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَجَعَلْناهُمْ سَلَفاً وَمَثَلاً لِلْآخِرِينَ** — **"And We made them a precedent and an example for the later [generations]."** (43:56). The precedent (السلف) is the one who goes before. It appears that their being a precedent for the later ones refers to their preceding them in entering the Fire. The example (المثل) is the current saying that is used as a parable and a lesson. It appears that their being an example for them means they are something from which the later ones should take a lesson, if they were to reflect and take heed.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ**, he said: "He did not make the speech plain."

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Abi Abdillah, reaching to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ**, he said: **إن الله لا يأسف كأسفنا ـ ولكنه خلق أولياء لنفسه يأسفون ويرضون ـ وهم مخلوقون مدبرون ـ فجعل رضاهم لنفسه رضى وسخطهم لنفسه سخطا ـ وذلك لأنه جعلهم الدعاة إليه والأدلاء عليه ـ فلذلك صاروا كذلك. وليس أن ذلك يصل إلى الله كما يصل إلى خلقه ـ ولكن هذا معنى ما قال من ذلك ، وقد قال أيضا من أهان لي وليا ـ فقد بارزني بالمحاربة ودعاني إليها ، وقال أيضا : مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ ، وقال أيضا : إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ وكل هذا وشبهه على ما ذكرت لك ، وهكذا الرضا والغضب وغيرهما من الأشياء مما يشاكل ذلك. ولو كان يصل إلى المكون الأسف والضجر ـ وهو الذي أحدثهما وأنشأهما ـ لجاز لقائل أن يقول : إن المكون يبيد يوما ـ لأنه إذا دخله الضجر والغضب دخله التغيير ـ فإذا دخله التغيير لم يؤمن عليه الإبادة ، ولو كان ذلك كذلك لم يعرف المكون من المكون ـ وإلا القادر من المقدور ولا الخالق من المخلوقين ـ تعالى الله عن هذا القول علوا كبيرا. هو الخالق للأشياء لا لحاجة ـ فإذا كان لا لحاجة استحال الحد والكيف فيه ـ فافهم ذلك إن شاء الله.** — "Indeed, Allah does not grieve (يأسف) as we grieve. Rather, He created friends (أولياء) for Himself who grieve and are pleased—and they are created and governed beings. He made their pleasure His pleasure and their wrath His wrath. This is because He made them the callers to Him and the guides toward Him; therefore, they became as such. It is not that this [emotion] reaches Allah as it reaches His creation, but this is the meaning of what He said regarding that. He also said: 'Whoever insults a friend of Mine has challenged Me to combat and invited Me to it.' And He said: 'Whoever obeys the Messenger has certainly obeyed Allah.' And He said: 'Indeed, those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah.' All of this and its like are according to what I have mentioned to you. Such is the case with pleasure, wrath, and other similar things. If grief and annoyance reached the Originator (المكون)—and He is the one who brought them into being and originated them—it would be permissible for a speaker to say: 'The Originator shall one day perish,' because if annoyance and wrath entered Him, change (التغيير) would enter Him. And if change entered Him, He would not be secure from perishing. If that were so, the Originator would not be distinguished from the originated, nor the Powerful from the powerless, nor the Creator from the created. Exalted is Allah far above such a saying. He is the Creator of things not out of need; and since it is not out of need, limit (الحد) and modality (الكيف) are impossible for Him. So understand that, if Allah wills."

I say: A similar narration is reported in *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Isma'il ibn Bazi' from his uncle Hamzah ibn Bazi' from him (عليه السلام).
